---
title: "Architecture: advanced"
description: "A developer's view: how the data object is organized, changes shape, fills up, and passes between components as it flows."
order: 70
---

There are two ways to read the pipeline. The [available
workflows](architecture.qmd) page walks it **by stage**, the way an analyst
thinks. This page reads it the way the workflows are **actually built**, the view
you need if you are writing or wiring components. It covers how the data object is
organized along **two axes** (samples and modalities), what gets **written into
it** at each step, and how components **hand data off** to one another.

## The data is organized along two axes

Two independent questions describe the object's shape at any point in the
pipeline: **how many samples** does it hold, and **how many modalities**. Steps
move the data along one axis or the other, and a given component only makes sense
at one position. The overview below shows both axes in play; the sections after
zoom in on each.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-data-states
%%| fig-cap: "The data moves through four shapes, one for each combination of the two axes (trapezoids). The axis-changing operations between them (blue) are the only steps that move data from one shape to the next; the processing done within each shape is noted inside it."
flowchart TD
  classDef state fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#70d287,color:#123
  classDef op fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000

  ingestion["Ingestion"]:::op
  ssmm[/"<b>single-sample · multimodal</b><br>one file per sample,<br>all modalities together"\]:::state
  splitmod["split modalities"]:::op
  ssum[/"<b>single-sample · unimodal</b><br>QC + filtering<br>per sample, per modality"\]:::state
  concatsamples["concatenate samples<br>adds sample_id"]:::op
  msum[/"<b>multi-sample · unimodal</b><br>normalize · select features · PCA"\]:::state
  mergemod["merge modalities"]:::op
  msmm[/"<b>multi-sample · multimodal</b><br>integration · clustering · UMAP"\]:::state

  ingestion --> ssmm --> splitmod --> ssum --> concatsamples --> msum --> mergemod --> msmm
```

### Samples: single-sample vs multi-sample

Per-sample work like QC and filtering reasons about a **single sample's own
distribution**, so it runs while each sample is still its own file. Cross-sample
work (normalization, feature selection, PCA) needs to see variation across the
**whole cohort**, so it runs only after the per-sample files are
**concatenated** into one.

Concatenation joins the observations of every sample into a single object, done
for each modality separately. A `sample_id` column is added to `.obs` to record
where each cell came from. When samples carry **conflicting** `.obs` / `.var`
annotations, the `concatenate_h5mu` component's default `other_axis_mode: move`
gathers the clashing columns into `.obsm` / `.varm` so you can inspect them
instead of silently dropping one.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-concatenate
%%| fig-cap: "Concatenation joins per-sample files into one cohort object, adding a sample_id column to .obs."
flowchart LR
  classDef obj fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#aaaaaa,color:#444
  classDef op  fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000

  s1["sample 1"]:::obj
  s2["sample 2"]:::obj
  s3["sample 3"]:::obj
  cc["concatenate<br>per modality"]:::op
  coh["cohort object<br>+ .obs sample_id"]:::obj

  s1 --> cc
  s2 --> cc
  s3 --> cc
  cc --> coh
```

### Modalities: multimodal vs unimodal

Ingestion produces a **multimodal** object: several modalities (`rna`, `prot`,
`atac`, ...) living side by side under `.mod`. But many per-modality steps
(RNA normalization, protein CLR, ...) only know how to operate on **one modality
at a time**. So the pipeline **splits** the multimodal object into one
**unimodal** file per modality, processes each on its own, then **merges** them
back into a single multimodal object. Split and merge are inverse operations
along the modality axis.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-split-merge
%%| fig-cap: "Split and merge are inverses along the modality axis: split_modalities produces one unimodal file per modality for per-modality processing; merge recombines them."
flowchart LR
  classDef obj fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#aaaaaa,color:#444
  classDef op  fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000

  mm["multimodal object<br>.mod: rna, prot, ..."]:::obj
  spl["split_modalities"]:::op
  urna["rna"]:::obj
  uprot["prot"]:::obj
  uother["..."]:::obj
  prna["processed rna"]:::obj
  pprot["processed prot"]:::obj
  pother["..."]:::obj
  mrg["merge"]:::op
  out["multimodal object<br>.mod: rna, prot, ..."]:::obj

  mm --> spl
  spl --> urna
  spl --> uprot
  spl --> uother
  urna --> prna
  uprot --> pprot
  uother --> pother
  prna --> mrg
  pprot --> mrg
  pother --> mrg
  mrg --> out
```

The same axis reappears at **integration**, where methods differ by how far
across modalities they reach:

- **Unimodal** integration aligns cells across batches within one modality:
  `integration/harmony_leiden`, `scvi_leiden`, `scanorama_leiden`, `bbknn_leiden`.
- **Multimodal** integration uses several modalities jointly:
  `integration/totalvi_leiden` (RNA + protein).
- **Mosaic** integration (batches and modalities where not every cell is profiled
  in every modality) is not currently available in OpenPipeline.

### Why the position matters

The `_singlesample` / `_multisample` suffixes throughout the codebase declare
which sample-axis position a workflow operates on. Combined with the modality
axis, they pin down exactly what a workflow assumes about its input.

| Shape | Samples | Modalities | Example workflows |
|---|---|---|---|
| single-sample · multimodal | one sample | all together | ingestion output; read by `dataflow/split_modalities` |
| single-sample · unimodal | one sample | one | `rna/rna_singlesample`, `prot/prot_singlesample` |
| multi-sample · unimodal | cohort | one | `rna/rna_multisample`, `prot/prot_multisample` |
| multi-sample · multimodal | cohort | all together | `integration/harmony_leiden`, ... (after `dataflow/merge`) |

The `multiomics` workflows (`process_samples`, and `process_batches` for large
cohorts) move data between these states, so you rarely wire the transitions by
hand.

## The object fills up as it flows

The object is never overwritten in place. Each step **adds** to it, writing its
result into a specific MuData slot (`.X`, `.layers`, `.obs`, `.var`, `.obsm`)
while leaving the earlier ones intact. Following which slot a step reads and which
it writes is often the fastest way to understand where it belongs.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-object-fills
%%| fig-cap: "The same object accumulating results. Each stage adds a slot rather than replacing the data; the raw counts in .X survive all the way through."
flowchart TD
  classDef add fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#70d287,color:#123

  ing["<b>Ingestion</b><br>.X raw counts<br>.obs barcodes<br>.var gene metadata<br>modalities under .mod"]:::add
  ss["<b>Single-sample QC + filter</b><br>+ .obs QC metrics &amp; filter flags"]:::add
  cat["<b>Concatenate</b><br>+ .obs sample_id"]:::add
  norm["<b>Log-normalize</b><br>+ .layers log_normalized"]:::add
  hvg["<b>Highly variable genes</b><br>+ .var highly_variable"]:::add
  pca["<b>PCA</b><br>+ .obsm X_pca"]:::add
  intg["<b>Integration</b><br>+ .obsm corrected embedding<br>+ .obs Leiden clusters<br>+ .obsm UMAP"]:::add
  ann["<b>Annotation</b><br>+ .obs cell-type predictions"]:::add

  ing --> ss --> cat --> norm --> hvg --> pca --> intg --> ann
```

The exact slot keys (all configurable, shown here with their defaults):

| Stage | Slots written |
|---|---|
| Ingestion | `.X` raw counts · `.obs` barcodes · `.var` gene metadata · modalities under `.mod` |
| Single-sample QC & filter | `.obs` QC metrics (`total_counts`, `num_nonzero_vars`, mitochondrial / ribosomal fraction) and filter flags |
| Concatenate | `.obs["sample_id"]` |
| Log-normalize | `.layers["log_normalized"]` |
| Highly variable genes | `.var["highly_variable"]` |
| PCA | `.obsm["X_pca"]` |
| Integration | corrected embedding in `.obsm` (e.g. `X_pca_integrated`), Leiden clusters in `.obs` (e.g. `harmony_integration_leiden`), UMAP in `.obsm` |
| Annotation | predicted cell types in `.obs` (default `<target>_pred`) |

Because raw counts stay in `.X` and each transform lands in its own slot, a
downstream step can always reach back to an earlier representation, and you can
re-run a step with different parameters without re-ingesting.

## Components exchange data through disk

Components do not share an in-memory object. Each one **reads an H5MU file from
disk, does its work, and writes a new H5MU back out**; the next component reads
that file in turn. This on-disk hand-off is what lets Nextflow run independent
branches in parallel, cache and resume completed steps, and wire **one-to-many**
(split one multimodal file into per-modality files) or **many-to-one**
(concatenate per-sample files into a cohort) relationships between steps. It also
means every intermediate is a real file you can open and inspect.

## When you author a component

Before adding a component to a workflow, decide where on the two axes it reads: a
**single sample** (so it belongs before concatenation, in a `_singlesample`
workflow) or the **whole cohort** (so it belongs after, in a `_multisample`
workflow), and **one modality** (so it runs after a split) or **all modalities
together** (so it runs after a merge). Those questions tell you where the
component slots in and which assumptions hold (is `sample_id` present yet? is the
data normalized? is this file unimodal?). See
[Create a pipeline](../contributing/creating-pipelines.qmd) for the mechanics of
wiring one in.
